pg407-p2-s1: The physician wasn’t hooked, he thought—he just prescribed drugs medically indicated for his many ailments.
pg407-p2-s2: Acceptance was his key to liberation.
pg407-p2: The physician wasn’t hooked, he thought—he just prescribed drugs medically indicated for his many ailments. Acceptance was his key to liberation.
pg407-p3-s1:
If there ever was anyone who came to A.A. by
mistake, it was I.
pg407-p3-s3:
Never
in my wildest moments had it occurred to me that I
might like to be an alcoholic.
pg407-p3-s4:
Never once had my
mother even hinted at the idea that, when I grew up,
I might like to be president of A.A.
pg407-p3-s5:
Not only did I not
think that being an alcoholic was a good idea, I didn’t
even feel that I had all that much of a drinking problem!
pg407-p3-s6:
Of course, I had problems, all sorts of problems.
“If you had my problems, you’d drink too” was my
feeling.
pg407-p3:
If there ever was anyone who came to A.A. by
mistake, it was I. I just didn’t belong here. Never
in my wildest moments had it occurred to me that I
might like to be an alcoholic. Never once had my
mother even hinted at the idea that, when I grew up,
I might like to be president of A.A. Not only did I not
think that being an alcoholic was a good idea, I didn’t
even feel that I had all that much of a drinking problem! Of course, I had problems, all sorts of problems.
“If you had my problems, you’d drink too” was my
feeling.
pg407-p4-s1:
My major problems were marital. “If you had my
wife, you’d drink too.” Max and I had been married
for twenty-eight years when I ended up in A.A.
pg407-p4-s2:
It
started out as a good marriage, but it deteriorated
over the years as she progressed through the various
stages of qualifying for Al-Anon.
pg407-p4-s3:
At first, she would
say, “You don’t love me.
pg407-p4-s4:
Why don’t you admit it?”
Later, she would say, “You don’t like me.
pg407-p4-s5:
Why don’t
you admit it?” And as her disease was reaching the
terminal stages, she was screaming, “You hate me!
pg407-p4-s7:
Why don’t you admit you hate me?” So I
admitted it.
pg407-p4:
My major problems were marital. “If you had my
wife, you’d drink too.” Max and I had been married
for twenty-eight years when I ended up in A.A. It
started out as a good marriage, but it deteriorated
over the years as she progressed through the various
stages of qualifying for Al-Anon. At first, she would
say, “You don’t love me. Why don’t you admit it?”
Later, she would say, “You don’t like me. Why don’t
you admit it?” And as her disease was reaching the
terminal stages, she was screaming, “You hate me! You
hate me! Why don’t you admit you hate me?” So I
admitted it.
